title: Layout Mastery
tagline: Control where everything goes on the page.

accomplishments: Make boxes sit side by side instead of stacking

do.title: Make boxes sit side by side
steps: Create a new file and save it as flexbox-intro.html
Copy and paste the code below
Save and open in browser
Boxes sit side by side instead of stacking!
do.code:
Flexbox Demo

Box 1
Box 2
Box 3
do.result: display: flex made boxes sit side by side instead of stacking!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing three boxes in a row.

concepts.name: display: flex
concepts.definition: Turns a container into a flex container. All direct children become flex items and line up in a row by default.
concepts.analogy: A shelf that automatically arranges books horizontally.

concepts.name: flex-direction
concepts.definition: row (default) = horizontal. column = vertical. row-reverse and column-reverse flip the order.
concepts.analogy: Choosing whether to stack books horizontally on a shelf or vertically in a pile.

concepts.name: gap
concepts.definition: Adds equal space between flex items without needing margins. gap: 20px puts 20px between each item.
concepts.analogy: Spacers between books on a shelf — keeps them evenly apart.

understand.keyTakeaway: display: flex + gap is all you need to make items sit side by side with space between them.

build.goal: Change direction to vertical and use flex: 1 for equal widths
build.hint: flex: 1 on each child makes them share space equally.

challenge.mission: Create a layout with header, three equal columns, and footer.
requirements: Different background colors for each section

Header

bridge.preview: In Lesson 2.2, you'll learn to space things out with justify-content and align-items — the two most powerful Flexbox properties!

lessons.title: Space Things Out with Justify and Align

accomplishments: Push items to opposite sides of a container

do.title: Space items apart
steps: Create a new file and save it as justify-align.html
Copy and paste the code below
Save and open in browser
See how each justify-content value arranges items differently!
do.code:
space-between

Item 1
Item 2
Item 3

center

Item 1
Item 2
Item 3

space-evenly

Item 1
Item 2
Item 3
do.result: Each justify-content value distributes items differently along the main axis!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing all three justify-content examples.

concepts.name: justify-content (main axis)
concepts.definition: Controls horizontal distribution: flex-start (left), center, flex-end (right), space-between (max gaps), space-around (equal around), space-evenly (equal everywhere).
concepts.analogy: Arranging photos on a wall — left-aligned, centered, spread out, or evenly spaced.

concepts.name: align-items (cross axis)
concepts.definition: Controls vertical alignment: stretch (fill height, default), flex-start (top), center (middle), flex-end (bottom).
concepts.analogy: Hanging pictures at different heights — top of wall, middle, bottom, or stretched to fill.

understand.keyTakeaway: justify-content = horizontal spacing. align-items = vertical alignment. Together they control exactly where items sit.

build.goal: Center items both horizontally and vertically
build.hint: justify-content: center + align-items: center + min-height: 100vh = perfect centering!

challenge.title: Build a Pricing Table
challenge.mission: Create three pricing cards side by side with centered content.
requirements: Each card has a title, price, features list, and button
Price should be large and bold
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add hover effect that scales the card up!

capabilities: Build pricing tables and card layouts

bridge.preview: In Lesson 2.3, you'll learn CSS Grid — the most powerful layout system for creating complex, two-dimensional layouts!

accomplishments: Create a grid layout with rows and columns
Make items span multiple columns or rows

steps: Create a new file and save it as css-grid.html
Copy and paste the code below
Save and open in browser
Items automatically arranged into 3 equal columns!
do.code:
CSS Grid Demo

1
2
3
4
5
6
do.result: Items automatically arranged into 3 equal columns!

concepts.name: display: grid and grid-template-columns
concepts.definition: display: grid activates Grid. grid-template-columns defines column widths. 1fr = 1 fraction of available space. repeat(3, 1fr) = three equal columns.
concepts.analogy: Drawing a table on paper — you define how many columns and how wide each one is.

concepts.name: auto-fit and minmax()
concepts.definition: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(250px, 1fr)) automatically adjusts the number of columns based on available space. No media queries needed!
concepts.analogy: A smart shelf that adds or removes dividers based on how wide it is.

concepts.name: Grid vs Flexbox
concepts.definition: Flexbox = one direction (row OR column). Grid = two directions (rows AND columns). Use Flexbox for components, Grid for page layouts.
concepts.analogy: Flexbox is a clothesline (one line). Grid is a chessboard (rows and columns).

understand.keyTakeaway: Grid is for two-dimensional layouts. auto-fit + minmax() creates responsive grids without media queries.

build.goal: Create a responsive grid with auto-fit
build.hint: 1fr means "one fraction of available space." Three 1fr columns means three equal columns.

challenge.title: Build a Dashboard Layout
challenge.mission: Create a dashboard with header, sidebar, main content, and footer using CSS Grid.
requirements: Header spans full width across top
Footer spans full width across bottom
challenge.bonusChallenge: Make the sidebar collapse on mobile using a media query!

capabilities: Create grid layouts with display: grid
Build responsive grids with auto-fit + minmax

bridge.preview: In Lesson 2.4, you'll learn CSS positioning — how to place elements exactly where you want them with sticky headers, fixed elements, and overlays.

lessons.title: Position Things Exactly Where You Want

accomplishments: Create a sticky header that follows you
Position elements on top of each other

do.title: Create a sticky header
steps: Create a new file and save it as positioning.html
Copy and paste the code below
Save and open in browser
Scroll down — the nav sticks to the top!
do.code:
Sticky Navigation

Scroll down...

The navigation stays at the top!
do.result: The navigation sticks to the top as you scroll!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the sticky nav at the top after scrolling.

concepts.name: Position values
concepts.definition: static (default, normal flow), relative (offset from normal), absolute (relative to positioned parent), fixed (relative to viewport), sticky (sticks when scrolling).
concepts.analogy: static = sitting in your assigned seat. relative = leaning in your chair. absolute = floating above the room. fixed = bolted to the wall. sticky = a magnet on a whiteboard.

concepts.name: z-index
concepts.definition: Controls stacking order. Higher z-index = appears on top. Only works on positioned elements (not static). Think of it as layers in Photoshop.
concepts.analogy: A stack of papers on a desk — higher z-index means the paper is on top of the pile.

concepts.name: Position absolute + relative
concepts.definition: Absolute positions relative to the nearest parent that has position: relative. This pattern lets you place elements precisely inside a container.
concepts.analogy: Absolute = placing a sticker. Relative parent = the page you're sticking it on.

understand.keyTakeaway: sticky for headers, fixed for modals, absolute + relative for precise placement inside containers.

build.goal: Create a modal overlay with fixed positioning
build.hint: Fixed positioning removes the element from normal flow and positions it relative to the viewport.

challenge.title: Build a Dismissible Banner
challenge.mission: Create an announcement banner that sticks to the top of the page with a close button.
requirements: Banner sticks to top with position: sticky
Close button in the top-right corner using position: absolute
Banner has a colored background with text
Proper z-index so it stays above content
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add JavaScript to actually hide the banner when clicking the close button!

capabilities: Create sticky headers with position: sticky
Place elements precisely with absolute + relative

bridge.preview: In Lesson 2.5, you'll learn display properties — the difference between block, inline, and inline-block, and how to fix common layout bugs.

accomplishments: See the difference between block, inline, and inline-block
Make images and text flow correctly

do.title: See display types in action
steps: Create a new file and save it as display-types.html
Copy and paste the code below
Save and open in browser
See how each display type behaves differently!
do.code:
Display Property Examples

Block Elements

Block 1
Block 2
Block 3

Inline Elements

Inline 1 Inline 2 Inline 3

Inline-Block Elements

Inline-Block 1
Inline-Block 2
Inline-Block 3
do.result: Block items stack vertically taking full width. Inline items flow like text. Inline-block items flow horizontally but respect width/height!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing all three display type examples.

concepts.name: Block Elements
concepts.definition: Take up the full width available and start on a new line. Default block elements: div, p, h1-h6, section, header, footer.
concepts.analogy: Shipping boxes stacked in a warehouse — each one takes up a full shelf.

concepts.name: Inline Elements
concepts.definition: Flow with text, only taking up as much width as their content needs. Width and height properties DON'T WORK on inline elements. Default inline elements: span, a, strong, em.
concepts.analogy: Words in a sentence — they flow together on the same line.

concepts.name: Inline-Block Elements
concepts.definition: The best of both worlds! Flow horizontally like inline, but respect width/height like block. Great for buttons, tags, and navigation items.
concepts.analogy: Books on a shelf — they sit side by side, but each book has its own defined size.

understand.keyTakeaway: Block stacks vertically (full width). Inline flows with text (no size control). Inline-block flows horizontally WITH size control.

build.goal: Fix the image gap bug and create a button group with inline-block
build.hint: Images are inline by default, which adds space for text descenders. Always set img { display: block; } to fix this!

challenge.title: Build a Tag Cloud
challenge.mission: Create a "tag cloud" of skill badges using inline-block — different sized badges that flow and wrap naturally.
requirements: Tags wrap to next line if container is narrow

Choose the right display property for each situation

bridge.preview: In Lesson 2.6, you'll combine everything you've learned — Flexbox, positioning, display properties — to build a professional navigation bar from scratch!

accomplishments: Build a real navigation bar from scratch

do.title: Build the desktop navigation
steps: Create a new file and save it as complete-nav.html
Copy and paste the code below
Save and open in browser
Hover over "Services" to see the dropdown
Scroll down — the nav sticks to the top!
do.code:
MyWebsite
  • Home
  • About
  • Services ▼
    Web Design Development Consulting
  • Portfolio
  • Contact

Professional Navigation Bar

Scroll down to see the navigation stick to the top!

Hover over "Services" to see the dropdown menu appear.

More content here...

Keep scrolling...

The navigation stays at the top as you scroll.

This is a common pattern on modern websites.

More content to make you scroll...

Almost there...

Last paragraph!
do.result: You built a complete, professional navigation bar with sticky positioning, dropdown menus, and smooth hover effects!
do.proofRequired: Screenshot showing the nav bar with dropdown menu visible.

concepts.name: Flexbox for navbar layout
concepts.definition: display: flex + justify-content: space-between puts the logo on the left and links on the right. align-items: center vertically centers everything.
concepts.analogy: Pushing two groups to opposite ends of a shelf — logo on one end, links on the other.

concepts.name: Sticky positioning
concepts.definition: position: sticky + top: 0 makes the navbar scroll normally until it hits the top, then it "sticks" there. z-index: 1000 keeps it above other content.
concepts.analogy: A fridge magnet — it slides around freely until it sticks to the top edge.

concepts.name: Dropdown pattern
concepts.definition: The dropdown is hidden (display: none) and positioned absolutely below its parent. On hover of the parent, it becomes visible (display: block).
concepts.analogy: A pull-down shade — it's rolled up and hidden until you pull the cord (hover).

concepts.name: Responsive mobile menu
concepts.definition: On mobile, the menu slides in from off-screen (right: -100% to right: 0) when a hamburger button is clicked. JavaScript toggles the .active class.
concepts.analogy: A sliding door that's hidden off to the side until you push a button to open it.

concepts.name: Combining CSS techniques
concepts.definition: Professional navbars use Flexbox (layout), positioning (sticky + absolute), display properties (block/none), transitions (smooth effects), and media queries (responsive) all together.
concepts.analogy: A recipe that uses multiple cooking techniques — searing, braising, and plating — to create one finished dish.

understand.keyTakeaway: A professional navbar combines Flexbox, positioning, display toggling, transitions, and media queries. Each technique solves one piece of the puzzle.

build.goal: Make the navigation responsive for mobile with a slide-in menu
build.hint: The menu starts off-screen (right: -100%) and slides in (right: 0) when active. The transition property makes it smooth!

challenge.title: Add Advanced Navigation Features
challenge.mission: Enhance your navigation bar with professional touches.
requirements: Smooth scroll to sections when clicking links
Close mobile menu when clicking a link
Add a subtle animation when page loads
Social media icons on the right side (desktop only)
Search bar that works on both desktop and mobile
challenge.starterCode:
<!-- Start with your complete-nav.html and add:

1. html { scroll-behavior: smooth; }
2. .nav-links a.active { background-color: #007bff; }
3. @keyframes slideDown { from { transform: translateY(-100%); } to { transform: translateY(0); } }
4. .navbar { animation: slideDown 0.5s ease; }

Then add JavaScript for:
- Toggle mobile menu on hamburger click
- Close menu when clicking a link on mobile
- Update active class based on scroll position
-->
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a "scroll to top" button that appears when you scroll down!

capabilities: Build complete navigation bars from scratch
Combine Flexbox, positioning, and display properties
Add smooth scroll and active page indicators
Use JavaScript to enhance CSS interactions

bridge.preview: Module 2 complete! In Module 3, you'll learn responsive design — making websites work beautifully on phones, tablets, and desktops using media queries, flexible units, and mobile-first thinking.
